concept: Language / The Whole of a People's Speech
description: A language: the whole system of a people's speech — its words, sounds, and ways. The verb 'haolu' (speak) sits complete inside it, grounded by 'ma': speaking made a whole. Not the tongue: 'lathoe' is anatomy only, and Phi does not copy English's body-metaphor for speech. Phi itself is a haoluma, and this word is how the language finally names its own kind.
sound_symbolism: The whole of 'haolu' flows first — open breath into liquid — and 'ma' grounds it, the way a shared language grounds the people who speak it. Four syllables of unhurried weight for a concept that deserves them.
grammatical_notes: Noun, counted with 'nophe': 'ta nophe haoluma' (one language). 'mia wi haoluma sano.' (I know two languages.) From the Babel text: 'theula muila ta haoluma to phelu.' (The whole earth held one language.)

pillars.peace-linguistics-practices: The field's own object, named at last: a language as a practice a people shares, not an instrument one wields. That the word grounds speech in 'ma' is the claim in miniature.

tags.community: What a people holds in common
